critic_raw: The projection-based truncated SVD update algorithm raises several methodological concerns. First, the theoretical efficiency claims require a careful analysis of when the projection-based update is actually faster than recomputing the full SVD. The paper presents complexity analyses but does not specify the conditions under which the projection approach achieves its stated speedup — specifically, for large rank-k updates or highly non-local matrix changes, the projection overhead may approach or exceed the full recomputation cost. Without characterizing the 'regime of advantage,' the efficiency claim is incomplete. Second, the accuracy of the truncated SVD update is measured as the approximation error relative to the exact truncated SVD of the updated matrix. The paper does not analyze error accumulation over many sequential updates — if the algorithm is applied repeatedly, projection approximation errors may accumulate and cause the maintained SVD to drift significantly from the true truncated SVD. Third, the comparison with baseline methods is limited in scope — the relevant comparison would include recent randomized SVD methods and streaming SVD algorithms. If the paper only compares against classical incremental SVD methods, the performance advantage may be overstated relative to the actual state of the art. Fourth, practical demonstration on real dynamic systems (e.g., streaming recommender systems, dynamic network analysis) is absent — all results use synthetically generated evolving matrices.
all_issues_raised: Regime of efficiency advantage over full SVD recomputation not characterized
Error accumulation over many sequential updates not analyzed
Comparison set may not include recent randomized or streaming SVD methods
No evaluation on real dynamic systems; only synthetic evolving matrices tested

round_transcripts.critic: The projection-based truncated SVD update algorithm raises several methodological concerns. First, the theoretical efficiency claims require a careful analysis of when the projection-based update is actually faster than recomputing the full SVD. The paper presents complexity analyses but does not specify the conditions under which the projection approach achieves its stated speedup — specifically, for large rank-k updates or highly non-local matrix changes, the projection overhead may approach or exceed the full recomputation cost. Without characterizing the 'regime of advantage,' the efficiency claim is incomplete. Second, the accuracy of the truncated SVD update is measured as the approximation error relative to the exact truncated SVD of the updated matrix. The paper does not analyze error accumulation over many sequential updates — if the algorithm is applied repeatedly, projection approximation errors may accumulate and cause the maintained SVD to drift significantly from the true truncated SVD. Third, the comparison with baseline methods is limited in scope — the relevant comparison would include recent randomized SVD methods and streaming SVD algorithms. If the paper only compares against classical incremental SVD methods, the performance advantage may be overstated relative to the actual state of the art. Fourth, practical demonstration on real dynamic systems (e.g., streaming recommender systems, dynamic network analysis) is absent — all results use synthetically generated evolving matrices.
round_transcripts.defender: On regime characterization: the paper provides asymptotic complexity bounds showing that the projection update has lower per-update cost than recomputing full SVD when the matrix dimensions are large relative to the rank k. This is the standard regime for truncated SVD applications. On error accumulation: the paper analyzes per-step approximation error; sequential error accumulation is bounded by the summation of per-step errors, which is characterized by the provided analysis. On comparison scope: the paper cites and compares against standard incremental SVD approaches; extending to all randomized methods would require a comprehensive survey. On real systems: synthetic experiments allow controlled characterization of the algorithm's properties without confounds from real-world data heterogeneity.

round_transcripts.critic: The defense's error accumulation response — that sequential error is bounded by summed per-step errors — is correct in a worst-case sense but does not address whether the accumulated error stays within a useful bound in practice. The error bound could be tight (accumulation is linear in number of updates) or loose (accumulation is sublinear due to self-correcting properties of the projection). Without empirical analysis of error over sequential update sequences, the practical accuracy guarantee is unclear. The real-systems gap remains — synthetic controlled experiments are appropriate for algorithmic characterization but the efficiency and accuracy claims should be validated on at least one real dynamic system to establish applicability. These are the two main unresolved concerns.
round_transcripts.defender: The sequential error accumulation gap and the absence of real-system validation are acknowledged as limitations. The algorithm's theoretical guarantees hold per-step, but empirical characterization of long-run accuracy is indeed needed for deployment confidence. These represent genuine gaps in the paper's experimental scope.

all_issues_adjudicated: Sequential error accumulation over many updates not empirically characterized
No validation on real dynamic systems
